basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Takāthur — “Vying for Increase” — is a Meccan sūra sometimes called by the Companions “the sūra of the grave”. The word takāthur (تَكَاثُر) is derived from kathīr (كَثِير), “much”: it names rivalry in possession, the desire to have more than another, and also to boast of it. Tradition reports that it was revealed about two clans of Quraysh competing over which had the larger tribe: they went to count their dead in the cemeteries to settle the dispute. The image is striking — vanity does not stop even at the graves. The sūra unfolds in two movements: a curt diagnosis (accumulation distracts until death), then a rising emphasis in which three degrees of knowing are named — knowledge, sight, experience — that later Sufi tradition will know as ʿilm al-yaqīn, ʿayn al-yaqīn, ḥaqq al-yaqīn: to know by indication, to see, and to live.

versets.commentaire: alhā (أَلْهَىٰ): to divert, to distract, to make one forget what truly counts. lahw (لَهْو), from the same root, names amusement, what occupies without nourishing. al-takāthur (التَّكَاثُر): rivalry in possession — not just hoarding, but hoarding so as to have more than another, and boasting of it. In six syllables the sūra states a law of the soul: what carries you toward hoarding carries you far from yourself.

mots.def: alhā: to divert, to distract, to occupy with trifles; perfect + plural suffix pronoun: “it has distracted you”.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: takāthur: reciprocal form of the root k-th-r; the race to hoard, rivalry by number and possession; also boasting about goods.

versets.commentaire: The verse can be read in two ways — and both carry. Literally, one visits the grave at one's own death: the distraction of having does not stop before the tomb. But the episode that surrounded the revelation gives it a sharper literal sense: those men had indeed gone to visit the graves to count their dead, as one counts one's goods. The verse turns them back upon themselves: you wanted to count the dead? Soon you will be counted among them.

mots.def: particle introducing a limit: until, up to.
zāra: to visit, to go to; perfect plural.
maqbara: cemetery, burial ground; in the plural, al-maqābir: the graves. Root q-b-r: to bury.

versets.commentaire: kallā (كَلَّا): “no!”, “by no means!” — one of the strongest particles of the Quran, abruptly denying what came before. sawfa (سَوْفَ): marks a distant, undetermined future. The verse does not argue; it posits a certainty. Commentators say this “you shall know” is fulfilled at several moments: at the instant of death, in the grave, on the Day of Judgment. The knowledge refused in life will come back into full light.

mots.def: strong refutation particle: nay!, by no means!.
particle of distant future: one day, in time to come.
ʿalima: to know; imperfect plural, 2nd pers.

versets.trad: Again, nay! Soon you shall know!
versets.commentaire: The near-identical repetition of the previous verse — with thumma (ثُمَّ), “then”, “again” — strengthens the warning. Some commentators hear in it two distinct moments: a first knowing at death (the grave), a second after the Resurrection. Others see only a doubling for whoever did not heed the first time.

mots.def: conjunction of temporal order: then, after that.
refutation particle.
future particle.
ʿalima: to know.

versets.trad: Nay! If you knew with the knowledge of certainty —
versets.commentaire: ʿilm al-yaqīn (عِلْم اليَقِين): the knowledge of certainty — the first of three degrees of yaqīn that Sufi tradition would distinguish: ʿilm al-yaqīn, knowing by indication or by trustworthy report (like knowing of a fire by seeing smoke); ʿayn al-yaqīn (v. 7), seeing the fire itself; ḥaqq al-yaqīn, dwelling in the fire — feeling its heat. The verse leaves the sentence hanging: “if you knew…”. The conditional answer is left in the air, so that each may complete it according to his own deeds.

mots.def: refutation particle.
conditional particle: if (irrealis).
ʿalima: to know, to be aware.
ʿilm: knowledge, science; in the construct accusative: “the knowledge of”.
yaqīn: certainty — not opinion, but what cannot be otherwise; in Sufism, a state of firm and doubtless knowing.

versets.commentaire: al-jaḥīm (الجَحِيم): Hellfire, one of the Quranic names for the Fire; from jaḥama (جَحَمَ), “to burn intensely”. The verse uses the energetic particle la-…-anna: “you would surely see it”. The hanging sentence of the previous verse begins to unfold.

mots.def: affirmative particle.
raʾā: to see; energetic imperfect, 2nd pers. plur.: “you would surely see”.
mots.gram: energetic imperfect verb, 2nd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: al-Jaḥīm: name of the Fire, the raging blaze; from the root j-ḥ-m, to burn intensely.

versets.trad: then you would surely see it with the eye of certainty;
versets.commentaire: ʿayn al-yaqīn (عَيْن اليَقِين): the eye of certainty — the second degree of yaqīn. If ʿilm al-yaqīn knew by indication, ʿayn al-yaqīn sees directly. Ibn ʿArabī, in his Meccan Openings, writes: “He who witnesses himself witnesses his Lord, and so moves from the certainty of knowledge to the certainty of the eye.” The verse implies that what was not taken seriously as knowing will be so as vision — and the difference is not slight.

mots.def: conjunction of temporal order: then.
affirmative particle.
raʾā: to see; energetic imperfect + feminine suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: energetic imperfect + suffix pronoun, 2nd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: ʿayn: the eye; by extension, direct vision, actual perception. In the construct accusative.
yaqīn: certainty; see v. 5.

versets.trad: then you will surely be questioned, on that Day, about ease and delight.
versets.commentaire: al-naʿīm (النَّعِيم): what makes life pleasant — goods, delights, ease, blessings. The sūra closes its loop: those goods one has piled up, one will be questioned about them. Not only about their amount, but about their use. The Prophet, recalling a meeting where Companions came to him after a hunger, said to them: “By the One in whose hand is my soul, you will be questioned about this blessing on the Day of Resurrection: hunger drew you from your homes, and you did not return before it reached you.” The smallest naʿīm will be named. And the first thing one will be questioned about, according to some commentators, will be the greatest grace of all — the Prophet himself.

mots.def: conjunction: then.
affirmative particle.
saʾala: to ask, to question; passive energetic imperfect plural: “you will surely be questioned”.
mots.gram: passive energetic imperfect, 2nd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: yawm + idh: on that day, on that very day; designates the Day of Judgment.
preposition ʿan: about, concerning.
naʿīm: ease, agreeableness, blessing; what makes life pleasant, from the smallest (cool water) to the greatest (prophethood, in some commentaries).
